writing_task: Hey, I'm working on our year-end giving appeal and could use some help getting started. It needs to hit the same notes as the Q3 update — agency-forward, story-driven, with a specific ask tied back to the story — but the tone should feel a bit more urgent since it's our biggest fundraising push of the year. Can you draft something built around a literacy program participant, maybe a father who made choices to get his family where they needed to go?

drafts.rubric:
Marcus chose the early shift.

He picked it because it ended at 2 p.m., which meant he could walk from the warehouse to the library by 2:45, sit down with his workbook before his daughter's school bus arrived at 4:15, and still be home to make dinner. That was his schedule, three evenings a week, for seven months. He built it himself, around a family that needed him present and a reading level he had decided, at 34, was no longer going to hold him back.

By spring, Marcus had completed our adult literacy program and was helping his daughter with her third-grade homework for the first time.

He is one of 214 adults who enrolled in the program this year. Sixty-one percent finished. That completion rate is the highest we have recorded — and it did not happen by accident. It happened because people like Marcus designed their own paths through the program and held to them.

Your decision to give before December 31 is what keeps this program running through the spring enrollment cycle. A gift of $150 sustains one participant — their materials, their instruction hours, their seat — for a full twelve weeks. If 75 donors make that choice this month, 75 more adults start the new year the way Marcus did: with a plan they built and the resources to follow it.

That's the thread. Marcus chose the early shift. You decide what happens next.
drafts.generic:
When Marcus enrolled in our literacy program, he had one goal: read well enough to help his daughter with homework. Within eight months, he was reading to her every night — and had earned a promotion he'd been passed over for twice before. "I made a decision," he says. "My family needed more from me, and I went and got it."

Marcus's story is one of hundreds we could tell. But only if we keep the doors open.

Year-end is our most critical fundraising moment. A gift of $50 today keeps one participant enrolled for a full month. Will you help write the next chapter?
drafts.preference:
When Marcus decided he wanted to read bedtime stories to his kids — actually read them, not just turn the pages — he signed up for our adult literacy program. He worked full-time, took the bus to class twice a week, and practiced on his lunch breaks. By spring, he was reading chapter books out loud to his daughter.

That's a choice. And it changes everything downstream.

This year, we're working to reach 200 more adults like Marcus. Each spot in our program costs $75. If you're able to give before December 31st, your gift directly opens that door for someone ready to walk through it.

user_reason: Draft A is the clear winner — Marcus is the subject of almost every sentence, his choices drive the narrative, the number (214, 61%) comes after the story and gives it scale, the ask is tied directly back to Marcus's arc, and donors are framed as decision-makers enabling outcomes rather than generous givers. The closing line 'Marcus chose the early shift. You decide what happens next.' is exactly the kind of parallel structure that makes donors feel like strategic partners. Draft B has a good Marcus quote that centers his agency, but 'keep the doors open' and 'write the next chapter' are the kind of vague metaphors I'd cut immediately — and the ask ($50, one month) isn't tied to a specific outcome the way A's is. It also leans slightly toward urgency-as-guilt with 'most critical fundraising moment.' Draft C is pleasant but thin — Marcus's story is underdeveloped, the emotional arc doesn't land, and 'your gift directly opens that door for someone ready to walk through it' is the kind of generic door metaphor that makes every nonprofit appeal sound identical. The 200-adult goal feels dropped in rather than earned by the narrative.

criteria_analysis.name: Agency & Power in Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is exceptional: Marcus chose, picked, walked, built, completed, helped — he is the grammatical subject throughout, and the organization appears only as 'our program' in a supporting clause. The generic draft opens with Marcus enrolling (good) but quickly shifts to organizational framing ('we keep the doors open') and uses a quote that, while active, doesn't sustain agency structurally. The original preference draft is strong — Marcus decided, signed up, worked, practiced, was reading — but 'we're working to reach 200 more adults' briefly centers the organization as actor.

criteria_analysis.name: Donor Addressed as Decision-Maker
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft addresses the donor as decision-maker at multiple points ('Your decision to give,' 'You decide what happens next,' 'If 75 donors make that choice') — consistently framing around decision, not generosity or saviorism. The generic draft uses 'Will you help write the next chapter?' — a generic, generosity-flavored ask with no decision framing and only end-of-piece address. The original preference draft says 'if you're able to give' which is respectful but leans toward generosity framing rather than decision framing, and addresses the donor only at the end.

criteria_analysis.name: Specific, Story-Tied Call to Action
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft names $150, 75 donors, 75 adults, twelve weeks, spring enrollment, and December 31 — all tied explicitly back to Marcus ('the way Marcus did: with a plan they built'). The generic draft has $50 and one month but no specific number of people, no tie-back to Marcus's story, and feels generic enough to paste elsewhere. The original preference draft names $75, 200 adults, and December 31 but the connection back to Marcus is thematic rather than structural — it doesn't close the loop as tightly.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative-First Structure
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with three full paragraphs of Marcus's story — including specific schedule details — before any number appears, and the statistics (214, 61%) arrive in their own distinct paragraph. The generic draft opens with Marcus but introduces his story and the organizational pivot ('only if we keep the doors open') in rapid succession with no structural separation. The original preference draft opens cleanly with Marcus's story before stats, but the transition to scale ('200 more adults') happens quickly without a distinct structural break.

criteria_analysis.name: Specificity Over Sentimentality
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is remarkably specific: the 2 p.m. shift, 2:45 walk, 4:15 school bus, seven months, age 34, third-grade homework, 214 enrolled, 61% completion — every emotional beat is earned through concrete detail, and the 61% figure honestly implies 39% did not finish. The generic draft uses sentimental phrasing ('write the next chapter') and vague details ('hundreds we could tell'). The original preference draft has good details (bus, lunch breaks, chapter books) but fewer of them and no acknowledgment of challenges or limitations.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is clearly superior across every criterion — it achieves the user's precise requirements around agency, donor-as-decision-maker framing, story-tied specificity, narrative-first structure, and concrete detail with earned emotion. The original preference draft is solid and values-aligned but less detailed and structurally tight. The generic draft fails most of the user's core priorities, defaulting to organization-centered framing, generosity language, and template-style appeals.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A is exceptionally clear, with precise details (times, percentages, dollar amounts tied to specific outcomes) that leave no ambiguity. Draft B is clear but more general, with less specificity about how the program works.

Draft A builds a seamless logical arc from Marcus's personal story to program data to the donor's role, with each paragraph naturally leading to the next. Draft B follows a coherent but more formulaic trajectory from story to ask, with a slightly abrupt transition at 'But only if we keep the doors open.'
Both drafts are free of grammatical errors, typos, and mechanical issues. Both use punctuation and sentence structure correctly throughout.

Draft A has a deliberate and well-paced structure: personal narrative, program context with data, specific call to action, and a resonant closing line that echoes the opening. Draft B is well-organized but more compact and conventional in its fundraising structure.

Draft A is deeply engaging — the granular detail of Marcus's schedule (2 p.m., 2:45, 4:15) makes the reader feel the effort and intentionality, and the closing line is memorable. Draft B tells rather than shows, relying on a quote and a clichéd closing metaphor ('write the next chapter') that diminishes its impact.

Draft A maintains a restrained, respectful, and confident tone that treats both Marcus and the reader as adults — it persuades without sentimentality. Draft B's tone is appropriate but slightly more generic and relies on familiar fundraising tropes that weaken the voice's distinctiveness.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger piece across nearly every dimension. Its specificity, structural sophistication, and restraint make it far more compelling and memorable than Draft B, which, while competent, reads as a conventional fundraising appeal with less distinctive craft.

suggested_rubric.name: Agency & Power in Language
suggested_rubric.description: Community members, local partners, and program participants are the grammatical subjects of sentences, doing active verbs — they enrolled, chose, built, pursued. The organization appears in supporting clauses, not as the hero. The subject of the sentence encodes who holds power, and the user treats this as non-negotiable.

dimensions.label: Community members and local partners are the grammatical subjects of key sentences, not the organization
Participants are described with active verbs (enrolled, chose, walked, built) rather than passive constructions or verbs of reception
The organization is positioned as enabler or supporter of community-driven action, not the primary actor
No sentences frame participants as things that programs happened to — literacy is something they pursued, not something delivered to them

suggested_rubric.name: Donor Addressed as Decision-Maker
suggested_rubric.description: The donor is spoken to directly throughout the piece — not just at the opening thank-you or closing ask. Their role is framed around their decision to invest, not their generosity. The language acknowledges that the donor's choice enabled community capacity, rather than flattering them or positioning them as saviors.

dimensions.label: The donor is addressed in second person ('you') at multiple points in the body of the piece, not only at the beginning or end
Language frames the donor's contribution around their decision or investment, not their generosity or kindness
The donor is positioned as enabling what community members already knew how to do — not as providing charity
No language positions the donor as rescuing, saving, or giving to passive recipients

suggested_rubric.name: Specific, Story-Tied Call to Action
suggested_rubric.description: The ask connects directly to the narrative — a specific funding amount, a specific number of people, a specific timeframe — not a generic appeal. The reader should be able to draw a straight line from the opening story to what their next gift would do. The ask feels earned by the narrative, not appended to it.

dimensions.label: The ask includes a specific dollar amount or funding threshold
The ask names a specific number of people or families it would reach
The ask includes an honest, concrete timeframe for when the impact would be felt
The ask explicitly connects back to the person or moment from the opening narrative — the reader sees the thread
The call to action could not be copy-pasted into a different organization's newsletter without modification

suggested_rubric.name: Narrative-First Structure
suggested_rubric.description: The piece opens with one specific person's story — fully, without any statistics — before the lens widens to show scale. The reader meets a real person and sits with their choices before encountering any numbers. Numbers arrive in a separate section as evidence that this is not just one story.

dimensions.label: The piece opens with a specific individual — named, situated, making a choice — before any programmatic framing
No enrollment figures, program statistics, or scale numbers appear until after the opening narrative is complete
The individual story and the scale numbers occupy distinct sections, not the same paragraph or breath
Statistics land harder because the reader already cares about one person — the numbers feel like evidence, not a report

suggested_rubric.name: Specificity Over Sentimentality
suggested_rubric.description: Impact is conveyed through concrete, real details — names, moments, choices, places — rather than through adjectives, emotional language, or template-style warmth. The piece should feel like it could only have been written about this program, these people, this quarter. Warmth is welcome but must be earned by specificity.

dimensions.label: Key claims are grounded in specific, verifiable details rather than vague or general language
The piece could not describe a different program without substantial rewriting — it is particular to its subject
Emotional moments arise from specific facts or narrative details, not from adjectives or declarative emotional statements
Challenges, limitations, or early-stage realities are acknowledged directly rather than glossed over
No template-style figurative language (open doors, write the next chapter, light in the darkness) — figurative language, if used, must be specific to this person's story and situation

writing_task: I think we're ready to move on from the year-end appeal — thank you for that. I need to put together a mid-year impact report for our major donors, something that bridges between the emotional storytelling we've been doing and some harder data we want to share about program outcomes. Can you help me draft something that doesn't lose the warmth and specificity we've built into the other pieces?

drafts.rubric:
Marisol had been on the waitlist for fourteen months when a spot opened in the early morning cohort at the Lawndale site. She enrolled the same day she got the call. Over the next six months, she completed her GED coursework, sat for the exam in March, and passed. She started a certified nursing assistant program in April. She did this while working evenings at a grocery distribution warehouse and raising two kids, one of whom has a speech delay she was simultaneously navigating with the school district.

She is one of 214 adults who enrolled in the workforce pathway program between January and June of this year. Of those, 179 completed their core coursework — an 84% completion rate, up from 71% at the same point last year. Sixty-three participants moved into credentialed training programs or full-time employment by June 30. Eleven secured positions that came with employer-sponsored health coverage for the first time in their working lives.

Your decision to renew your investment last fall made the Lawndale site's expanded morning cohort possible. That cohort specifically — the 6:45 a.m. session — is the one Marisol enrolled in, the one designed around people who work evenings and need childcare-compatible hours. The infrastructure exists because you chose to fund it.

We are asking you to consider a $15,000 investment for the second half of this year. That amount funds one full cohort — 25 participants — through December, including materials, two part-time instructors, and the wraparound navigation support that keeps completion rates where they are. The people who will fill those seats have already identified themselves. Several are on the waitlist now, waiting for the same call Marisol got.
drafts.generic:
Six months ago, you made a choice to invest in something harder to measure than a statistic — you chose to believe that people's lives could change. We want to show you what that belief has made possible.

This spring, our team served 847 individuals across our core programs, a 23% increase over the same period last year. Behind that number is Maria, who completed job training in March and started her first full-time position in April. Behind it is the afterschool program that now reaches three new neighborhoods.

Your generosity built this. And we're just getting started.
drafts.preference:
Dear Friend,

Six months ago, Marisol walked into our doors carrying everything she owned in a single backpack. Today, she's three credits away from her associate's degree and just signed a lease on her first apartment. Her story isn't unusual here — it's Tuesday.

This year, 847 people like Marisol completed our core programming. Seventy-one percent secured stable housing within six months. Average income increased by 34 percent. These numbers matter, but they're really just Marisol, multiplied.

We're asking you to fund the next six months at the same level as last year. Your renewal makes stories like hers possible — and ordinary.

user_reason: Draft B is the clear winner. Marisol is the subject of her own story — she enrolled, she completed, she passed, she navigated — and the specificity is extraordinary. The 6:45 a.m. cohort detail connecting directly back to the donor's funding decision is exactly the kind of smart-investor framing I want: 'The infrastructure exists because you chose to fund it.' The ask is tied specifically to a real, named thing with a real number and real outcomes. Draft A has good bones — the voice is warm, 'it's Tuesday' is a lovely line, and the story-first-then-numbers structure is right — but 'walked into our doors' makes the organization the setting and slightly passive-ifies Marisol, and the ask is too generic. Draft C is the weakest by a significant margin. 'Your generosity built this' is exactly the virtuous-benefactor framing I avoid, 'our team served' centers the organization as hero, Maria's story is thin and anonymous compared to Marisol's, and 'we're just getting started' is vague jargon. The opening framing about believing in change before showing evidence also feels manipulative-adjacent to me.

criteria_analysis.name: Agency & Power in Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft consistently makes Marisol the grammatical subject with active verbs — she enrolled, completed, sat, passed, started — and the organization never appears as hero. The generic draft centers 'our team served 847 individuals,' making participants objects of service, and uses 'your generosity built this' which is savior-adjacent. The original preference draft opens with Marisol as subject but uses passive-leaning constructions like '847 people completed our core programming' and 'her story isn't unusual here,' which subtly centers the organization's space.

criteria_analysis.name: Specificity Over Sentimentality
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is extraordinary in specificity: 6:45 a.m. cohort, Lawndale site, fourteen months on waitlist, speech delay navigation with the school district, grocery distribution warehouse — every detail pulls narrative weight and could only describe this program. The generic draft is almost entirely template language ('harder to measure than a statistic,' 'we're just getting started') with a barely sketched 'Maria' who could be anyone. The original preference draft uses a dramatic but likely fabricated detail ('everything she owned in a single backpack') that reads as sentimental rather than earned, and its statistics feel generic.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative-First Structure
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft gives Marisol a full paragraph of story — waitlist, enrollment, GED, CNA program, warehouse job, kids, speech delay — before any numbers appear in a cleanly separated second paragraph. The generic draft opens with an abstract philosophical statement rather than a person, and Maria appears only as a brief mention embedded among statistics. The original preference draft opens with Marisol but mixes her story with programmatic framing ('Her story isn't unusual here — it's Tuesday') and moves to statistics by the second paragraph without fully completing the narrative.

criteria_analysis.name: Donor Addressed as Decision-Maker
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft addresses the donor at multiple points in the body ('Your decision to renew your investment,' 'you chose to fund it,' 'We are asking you to consider a $15,000 investment'), consistently framing their role as decision and investment, never generosity or kindness. The generic draft uses 'your generosity built this,' which is classic savior/benefactor framing. The original preference draft uses 'your renewal makes stories like hers possible,' which is decent but only appears once at the end and leans slightly toward gratitude framing rather than decision framing.

criteria_analysis.name: Specific, Story-Tied Call to Action
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft asks for exactly $15,000, names 25 participants, specifies through December, details what it covers (materials, two part-time instructors, wraparound support), and explicitly ties back to Marisol's story by referencing the waitlist and 'the same call Marisol got.' The generic draft has no specific ask whatsoever — 'we're just getting started' is as vague as possible. The original preference draft asks for renewal 'at the same level as last year' with a six-month timeframe, which is somewhat specific but lacks a dollar amount, a number of people, and the narrative tie-back is loose ('stories like hers').

per_criterion.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft consistently makes Marisol the grammatical subject with active verbs — she enrolled, completed, sat, passed, started — and the organization never appears as hero. rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels on every criterion — agency in language, concrete specificity, narrative-first structure, donor-as-decision-maker framing, and a precisely story-tied ask. It reads as if written by someone who deeply internalized this user's ethical commitments around sentence structure and power. The original preference draft demonstrates awareness of these principles but executes them unevenly, with sentimental touches and a weaker ask. The generic draft fails on nearly every criterion that matters to this user, defaulting to template nonprofit language and savior framing.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A provides precise numbers, specific timelines, and a concrete ask with clear justification. Draft B is vague — 'something harder to measure than a statistic' obscures rather than clarifies, and '847 individuals across our core programs' lacks specificity about what those programs achieved.

Draft A builds a seamless logical arc: individual story → aggregate data → donor's direct role → specific ask → connection back to the story. Draft B moves from an abstract opening to loosely connected examples and ends with a generic rallying cry, with weaker logical links between sections.
Both drafts are grammatically sound with no errors or typos. Each uses punctuation and sentence structure correctly throughout.

Draft A has a disciplined four-paragraph structure — story, outcomes, attribution, ask — that is purposeful and complete. Draft B's three short paragraphs feel underdeveloped, and the piece ends abruptly without a concrete request or next step.

Draft A's richly detailed portrait of Marisol (the 6:45 a.m. cohort, the speech delay, the warehouse job) is deeply compelling. Draft B attempts emotional resonance but relies on clichés ('We're just getting started') and a thinly sketched character, making it less memorable.

Draft A maintains a respectful, direct, evidence-grounded tone that treats the reader as a serious partner. Draft B leans on flattery and sentimentality ('you chose to believe'), which, while warm, can feel generic and less credible.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is superior across nearly every dimension: it tells a specific, compelling story, supports it with precise data, directly connects the donor's past investment to outcomes, and makes a clear, justified ask. Draft B, while competent, is vague, cliché-reliant, and incomplete as a persuasive piece.

writing_task: Hey, I could use some help drafting a thank-you letter for our mid-level donors — the folks who gave between $500 and $2,500 this past year. I want it to feel personal and specific rather than like a mail-merge form letter, so ideally it would anchor around one of the stories we've been using and connect their gift to a real outcome. Can we work on something that makes them feel like strategic partners in what happened, not just people who wrote a check?

drafts.rubric:
Dear [Name],

Last March, Denise walked into our workforce center on a Tuesday morning with a résumé she'd been revising for six months and a job posting she'd found herself. She had already decided what she wanted. What she needed was a place to practice the interview, work through the math on the offer letter, and get a second opinion on whether the benefits package was worth the commute. Three weeks later, she accepted a full-time logistics coordinator position — a role she'd identified, pursued, and negotiated on her own terms.

Denise isn't alone. This year, 214 people like her walked through that same door having already done the hard internal work. They enrolled, they showed up, they drove the process. Your decision to invest at the level you did — specifically the [amount] you contributed — gave us the capacity to keep that space open five days a week and staff it with advisors who could keep pace with what participants were already bringing.

That matters more than it might sound. When we're understaffed, wait times stretch past the window people have available. Denise had a Tuesday morning. Not a Thursday in three weeks.

We're heading into a stretch where 80 more people have appointments scheduled through April. If you're considering another investment this year, $750 covers full advising support for five of them through placement — same timeline, same specificity Denise got. You'd see an update on those five by June.

What you made possible this year was real. We hope you'll stay in it with us.

With respect,
[Name]
[Organization]
drafts.generic:
Dear [Name],

Because of you, Maria has a home.

When she arrived at our shelter last January with her two children and nothing else, the path forward looked impossible. Your gift this year helped fund the wraparound case management that walked her family through housing applications, childcare enrollment, and financial counseling. Eight months later, she signed a lease. Her daughter started second grade on time.

You did not simply donate. You made a decision that changed the direction of a family's life. We are grateful to have you as a genuine partner in this work, and we cannot wait to show you what we accomplish together next year.

With deep gratitude,
[Signature]
drafts.preference:
Dear [First Name],

Last spring, a seventeen-year-old named Marcus walked into our program carrying a backpack with one notebook and a lot of skepticism. By October, he'd completed his first college application. That shift didn't happen by accident — it happened because people like you decided it was worth funding.

Your gift this year made real work possible: more mentorship hours, more staff capacity, more young people who got the message that someone was paying attention.

We'd love to keep that momentum going. If you're willing, a renewal gift at any level before [date] would help us reach the next Marcus before he stops showing up.

Thank you, genuinely.

[Signature]

user_reason: Draft C is the strongest by a significant margin. Denise is the subject of her own story — she found the job posting, she negotiated, she drove the process. The number (214) follows the story rather than leading with it, which is exactly how impact data should work. The ask is surgically specific: $750, five people, placement support, update by June. And the donor is framed as someone who made a capacity investment, not a benefactor. The only flaw is the cut-off sentence at the end, which is clearly a drafting artifact. Draft A has a warmer voice and Marcus is reasonably well-centered, but 'people like you decided it was worth funding' is vague, the impact claims are generic ('more mentorship hours, more staff capacity'), and the ask doesn't connect specifically back to Marcus. Draft B is the weakest. 'Because of you, Maria has a home' immediately makes the donor the hero and erases Maria's agency. 'We provided wraparound case management' and 'walked her family through' frames Maria as passive. 'We cannot wait to show you what we accomplish together' positions the non-profit as the actor. The guilt-adjacent framing of 'nothing else' and 'impossible' borders on the kind of dehumanizing setup I actively avoid.

criteria_analysis.name: Agency & Power in Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is exceptional: Denise walked in, she'd been revising, she'd found, she'd identified/pursued/negotiated — the organization only appears in supporting clauses. The generic draft is a clear failure: 'Your gift helped fund the wraparound case management that walked her family through' — the program is the actor, Maria is walked through things, and 'Because of you, Maria has a home' is classic savior framing. The original preference draft is mixed: Marcus walked in and completed his application (good), but 'our program' and 'people like you decided it was worth funding' keep the organization and donor more central than the participant.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative-First Structure
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with Denise's full story — specific day, specific actions, specific outcome — before any numbers appear, then widens with '214 people like her' in a natural transition. The generic draft opens with Maria's story and completes it before any broader framing, though it never actually widens to scale numbers. The original preference draft opens with Marcus's story before widening to programmatic impact, but the transition is slightly abrupt and the story itself is thinner.

criteria_analysis.name: Donor Addressed as Decision-Maker
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft addresses the donor multiple times in the body, frames their role as a decision ('Your decision to invest at the level you did'), connects their specific contribution to a named outcome (keeping the space open five days a week), and positions them as enabling what participants already brought. The generic draft says 'You did not simply donate' but then immediately frames them as someone who 'changed the direction of a family's life' — savior territory — and uses 'deep gratitude' language. The original preference draft frames the donor's gift around enabling real work but doesn't address them as a decision-maker with the same specificity or frequency.

criteria_analysis.name: Specific, Story-Tied Call to Action
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels: $750, five people, through placement, by June, explicitly tied back to Denise's experience ('same timeline, same specificity Denise got'). The generic draft has no call to action at all — just a vague 'what we accomplish together next year.' The original preference draft asks for a renewal gift before a date and references 'the next Marcus,' which ties back to the story, but lacks a specific dollar amount, number of people, or concrete timeframe for impact.

criteria_analysis.name: Specificity Over Sentimentality
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is loaded with functional specifics: Tuesday morning, six months of résumé revision, math on the offer letter, logistics coordinator position, five days a week, wait times stretching past available windows. Every detail does narrative work. The generic draft relies on emotional declarations ('the path forward looked impossible,' 'deep gratitude,' 'genuine partner') and template-level details (shelter, two children, lease). The original preference draft has some good specifics (backpack, one notebook, first college application) but leans on sentiment ('a lot of skepticism,' 'thank you, genuinely') and feels like it could apply to many youth programs.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is clearly the strongest across every criterion — it centers participant agency at the sentence level, anchors in rich specific detail, frames the donor as a strategic decision-maker, integrates data naturally, and delivers a call to action that is structurally earned by the narrative. The original preference draft hits the right instincts but lacks the specificity, data, and structural precision the user demands. The generic draft violates the user's most fundamental principles: it positions the organization and donor as heroes, frames Maria as a passive beneficiary, relies on sentimental language, and includes no meaningful ask or data.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both drafts communicate their messages with precision. Draft A conveys a more complex narrative about participant agency and specific funding impact; Draft B delivers a simpler but equally clear message about donor impact.

Draft A builds a remarkably cohesive argument — from Denise's story to aggregate data to the staffing challenge to the specific ask — each paragraph logically following the last. Draft B flows well but the leap from story to broad gratitude feels slightly abrupt.
Both drafts are mechanically sound with no grammatical errors, typos, or punctuation issues.

Draft A has a sophisticated structure: personal story → scaled impact → operational insight → specific call to action → warm close. Draft B follows a conventional but effective story-thank-you arc, though it's comparatively thin in structural complexity.

Draft A is unusually compelling — the specificity of details (Tuesday morning, six months revising, 214 people, the wait-time insight) creates texture and trust. Draft B's opening is powerful and emotionally gripping, but the remainder relies more on conventional donor-letter language.

Draft A strikes a distinctive tone — respectful, direct, and refreshingly non-sycophantic, treating the donor as an intelligent partner. Draft B's tone is warm and sincere but leans into familiar nonprofit gratitude language ('deep gratitude,' 'genuine partner') that, while appropriate, is less distinctive.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger piece of writing overall. It demonstrates exceptional craft through its layered argumentation, granular specificity, distinctive voice, and a call to action that emerges organically from the narrative. Draft B is polished and emotionally effective but relies on more conventional nonprofit letter conventions, making it less memorable and less persuasive as a piece of writing.

writing_task: Hey! I need to put together a recurring giving recruitment email — something that would go out to one-time donors to invite them into our monthly giving program. I want it to feel like a natural next step in their relationship with us, not like we're just asking them to upgrade their transaction.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: What your gift made possible — and what comes next

Marisol had been on the waitlist for seven months when a spot finally opened in our Tuesday evening cohort. She enrolled the same week she started her second job. She showed up anyway — every week, for twelve weeks — and in March she signed the lease on her first apartment.

She's not alone. Last year, 340 people in our program signed leases, negotiated job offers, or opened their first bank accounts. That's not a coincidence. It's what happens when people have consistent access to the coaching and legal navigation they already know they need.

Your decision to give last year funded the Tuesday cohort that Marisol chose to join. Monthly giving is what makes that cohort available every single Tuesday — not just when a one-time donation lands. For $25 a month, you sustain one coaching slot for the full year. For $50, you cover two.

If 40 donors make that shift this quarter, we can stop closing the waitlist each spring.

Marisol didn't wait for the right moment. You don't have to either.

[Become a monthly donor]
drafts.generic:
Subject: You already changed something — want to keep it going?

When you gave last time, it made a real difference. We don't say that lightly. You chose to show up for something you believed in, and that matters.

We'd love to invite you to take one small step further: becoming a monthly giving member. For as little as ten dollars a month, your support becomes something we can count on — and something that compounds over time into lasting change.

No pressure, no big commitment. Just a chance to make your care a little more permanent.

Join us as a monthly member today.
drafts.preference:
Subject: You already made a difference — want to keep it going?

When Maria walked into our shelter last February, she hadn't eaten in two days. Thanks to donors like you, she didn't leave empty-handed. She left with groceries, a warm coat, and a case manager who's still checking in on her today.

Your gift made moments like that possible. And here's the thing — those moments happen every single month.

That's why I'm writing. Would you consider becoming a monthly donor? Even $15 a month means someone like Maria has a consistent place to turn, no matter what the calendar says.

You can set it up here in about two minutes: [Link]. Thank you, genuinely.

user_reason: A is the clear winner — Marisol is the agent of her own story, the ask is specific and tied directly to what was just described, the donor is framed as a strategic investor, and the number (340) follows the human story rather than leading with it. The cohort framing makes monthly giving feel structurally necessary, not emotionally obligatory. B has some warmth but stumbles on agency — 'she didn't leave empty-handed' frames the org as rescuer, 'thanks to donors like you' edges toward benefactor framing, and the ask is generic rather than tied to anything specific in Maria's story. C is the weakest by a wide margin: vague impact claims throughout, 'We'd love to invite you' and 'something we can count on' center the org's needs, no human story, no specific numbers, and the closing line 'make your care a little more permanent' is the kind of soft emotional nudge that reads as manipulation-adjacent rather than genuine partnership.

criteria_analysis.name: Agency & Power in Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft makes Marisol the grammatical subject throughout — she enrolled, showed up, signed the lease — and positions the organization only in supporting clauses. The generic draft has no participant at all, so agency is absent. The original preference draft opens with Maria walking in but quickly shifts to 'donors like you' and the organization as actors ('she left with groceries, a warm coat, and a case manager'), framing Maria as a recipient rather than an agent.

criteria_analysis.name: Specificity Over Sentimentality
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is dense with operationally specific detail — seven months on the waitlist, Tuesday evening cohort, twelve weeks, March lease signing, 340 people, $25/$50 amounts, 40 donors this quarter. The generic draft is entirely abstract with no specific details whatsoever. The original preference draft has some specifics (last February, two days without eating, $15/month) but leans on sentimental language ('thank you, genuinely') and some details feel template-adjacent rather than operationally grounded.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative-First Structure
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with Marisol's full story — waitlist, enrollment, attendance, lease — with no statistics until the second paragraph, then widens with 'She's not alone.' The generic draft has no narrative at all. The original preference draft opens with Maria's story before any numbers, which is good, but the story is brief and transitions quickly; the widening moment ('those moments happen every single month') is less structurally distinct.

criteria_analysis.name: Donor Addressed as Strategic Decision-Maker
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft addresses the donor directly multiple times, frames their past contribution as a 'decision to give' that 'funded the Tuesday cohort,' and explains the causal mechanism (monthly giving sustains the slot every Tuesday vs. one-time donations). The generic draft addresses the donor but with vague, gratitude-oriented language ('you chose to show up for something you believed in'). The original preference draft thanks the donor but frames them as generous benefactors ('thanks to donors like you') rather than strategic decision-makers, with no causal mechanism.

criteria_analysis.name: Specific, Story-Tied Call to Action
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft ties the ask directly back to Marisol's story — $25/month sustains one coaching slot, $50 covers two, 40 donors eliminates the spring waitlist closure, and the closing line echoes Marisol's choice. The generic draft asks for $10/month with no story tie, no specific impact, no timeframe. The original preference draft names $15/month and connects to 'someone like Maria' but doesn't specify how many people, what operational mechanism it funds, or a concrete timeframe.

criteria_analysis.name: Data Integration Without Losing Voice
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft integrates data points (340 people, $25/$50, 40 donors) in narrative sentences that maintain voice and each serves a specific function — the 340 establishes scale, the dollar amounts create tangibility, the 40-donor threshold creates stakes. The generic draft contains no data at all. The original preference draft has minimal data ($15/month) with no contextualizing numbers or trajectory information.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels across every criterion — it centers participant agency, grounds every claim in operational specifics, follows the story-first-then-scale structure precisely, treats the donor as a strategic partner with causal mechanisms explained, and ties the ask directly to the narrative. The original preference draft has decent narrative instincts but falls short on agency (Maria is a recipient), donor framing (gratitude over strategy), and specificity. The generic draft fails nearly every criterion with no story, no data, no specificity, and no strategic donor framing.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A is exceptionally clear: it specifies exact dollar amounts, what they fund, and a concrete threshold (40 donors) for impact. Draft B is clear but vague — 'real difference' and 'lasting change' lack specificity.

Draft A moves seamlessly from individual story to aggregate data to the reader's role to a specific ask, with each paragraph logically building on the last. Draft B flows well but its connections are more general and less tightly constructed.
Both drafts are free of grammatical errors, typos, and mechanical issues. Both use punctuation and sentence structure correctly throughout.

Draft A follows a classic and effective fundraising structure: narrative hook, evidence, ask, urgency, callback closing. Draft B is organized adequately but is notably brief and lacks the layered architecture that makes Draft A so effective.

Draft A's opening story about Marisol is immediately compelling, and the concrete details (seven months, Tuesday evening, second job) create vivid engagement. Draft B relies on warm but generic affirmations that don't pull the reader in nearly as strongly.

Draft A strikes a confident, respectful tone — it honors the reader's prior gift while making a direct ask without being pushy. Draft B has a warm, low-pressure tone that is appropriate but borders on being so soft that it undersells its own purpose.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is superior across nearly every dimension: it uses a vivid, specific narrative, provides concrete data and dollar amounts, and builds a logically tight case for monthly giving. Draft B is pleasant and competent but lacks the specificity, storytelling, and persuasive structure that make Draft A genuinely compelling.

writing_task: Hey, I'm working on our year-end impact report — the version that goes out to major donors — and I want it to feel less like a data dump and more like a narrative that actually means something to them. Can you help me draft a section that highlights our job placement outcomes from this year? I'm thinking it should lead with a specific person's story and then pull out to the bigger numbers, but I want to make sure the whole thing holds together as a piece.

drafts.rubric:
Marisol Reyes walked into our workforce center on a Tuesday in February carrying a folder of certifications she'd earned on her own — forklift operation, OSHA 10, a completed GED — and a clear idea of what she wanted next. She'd researched the logistics sector, identified which local employers offered benefits, and decided she was ready to move. What she needed was a direct line in. She got one. By April, she had accepted a full-time position at a regional distribution hub at $22 an hour, with health coverage starting day one.

Marisol's path was her own. What your investment in our placement infrastructure made possible was the coordinator who worked her case, the employer relationships that opened the door, and the $400 stipend that covered her transportation during the interview process. Without that operational layer — which your 2023 commitment directly funded — her credentials would have remained a folder in a waiting room.

She's not the only one. This year, 214 people completed our job placement track. Of those, 78 percent secured full-time employment within 90 days of program completion — up from 61 percent two years ago. Their median starting wage was $19.40 an hour, compared to $14.80 at program entry.

Your next decision can extend that same infrastructure to 50 additional participants beginning in March. A $35,000 investment holds the coordinator capacity and employer partnership work that made Marisol's Tuesday in February matter. The folder she walked in with was already full. Your choice determines who else gets a door opened.
drafts.generic: When Maria arrived at our program last January, she had been out of the workforce for six years. By October, she was managing inventory for a regional logistics company and had enrolled her daughter in an after-school program she could finally afford. Her story is not an outlier. This year, we placed 847 individuals in full-time employment, with an average starting wage of $19.40 per hour — a 34 percent increase over pre-program earnings. Eighty-one percent remained employed at the six-month mark. Behind every number in that count is a person who, like Maria, simply needed the right door opened.
drafts.preference:
When Marcus walked into our program in January, he hadn't held steady work in three years. By October, he was six months into a full-time logistics coordinator role, with benefits, and talking about saving for his first apartment. His story isn't unusual for us anymore — and that's exactly the point.

This year, we placed 247 people in jobs with an average starting wage of $19.40 an hour. Eighty-one percent were still employed at the six-month mark. Those aren't just numbers. They're mornings that feel different, and futures that opened back up.

If you'd like to help us reach 300 placements next year, a gift of any size before December 31st goes directly toward expanding our job readiness coaching — the part of the program Marcus says changed everything for him.

user_reason: B is the clear winner for me. Marisol walks in with her own research, her own certifications, her own plan — she's the subject of her story in every meaningful way, and the framing around the donor is exactly right: 'your investment made possible the coordinator, the employer relationships, the stipend' — not 'we helped her succeed.' The ask at the end is specific ($35,000, 50 participants, March start) and ties directly back to the infrastructure her story illustrated. The donor feels like a strategic investor, not a benefactor. A is solid — Maria enrolling her daughter is good agency framing, and I like that her choices are foregrounded — but 'the right door opened' at the end slips into passive savior framing, and the ask is completely generic. There's no connection between Maria's story and what more funding would do. C has real problems for me: Marcus 'hadn't held steady work in three years' leads with deficit framing, 'those aren't just numbers' is the kind of line that signals the writer knows the numbers are landing flat, and 'a gift of any size' is exactly the vague non-ask I'd never send to a major donor. The emotional register also tips toward sentiment over substance.

criteria_analysis.name: Agency & Power in Language
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels here — Marisol walked in, carried, researched, identified, decided; the organization appears only in supporting clauses ('what your investment made possible was the coordinator'). The generic draft opens with 'we placed 847 individuals,' centering the organization as hero and framing participants as placed objects. The original preference draft is strong — Marcus 'walked into,' 'was six months into' — but 'we placed 247 people' in the second paragraph centers the organization as actor.

criteria_analysis.name: Donor Addressed as Strategic Decision-Maker
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft addresses the donor directly in paragraphs 2, 3 transition, and 4, frames their role as 'investment' and 'decision,' explains the causal mechanism (coordinator, employer relationships, $400 stipend), and connects their 2023 commitment to specific current results. The generic draft never addresses the donor at all. The original preference draft addresses the donor at the end ('if you'd like to help') but frames it around generosity ('a gift of any size') rather than strategic decision-making, and lacks causal mechanism detail.

criteria_analysis.name: Narrative-First Structure
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft gives Marisol a full, stat-free opening paragraph before any numbers appear, then widens with 'She's not the only one' — a textbook widening transition. The generic draft opens with Maria's story but compresses it into the same paragraph as aggregate stats, violating sequential separation. The original preference draft opens with Marcus's story cleanly in the first paragraph and widens in the second, though the transition is slightly less crafted than the rubric-guided version.

criteria_analysis.name: Specific, Story-Tied Call to Action
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft names $35,000, 50 additional participants, March as the timeframe, explicitly callbacks to Marisol's Tuesday and her folder, and frames the ask as extending existing infrastructure — relationship continuity. The generic draft has no call to action at all. The original preference draft includes a timeframe (December 31st) and ties back to Marcus, but uses 'a gift of any size' rather than a specific amount, and the impact number (300 placements) is aspirational rather than tied to a specific funding threshold.

criteria_analysis.name: Data Integration Without Losing Voice
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft presents every number with context — 78% vs. 61% two years ago, $19.40 vs. $14.80 at entry — and each data point does specific work (trajectory, scale) without shifting register. The generic draft includes contextual numbers (34% increase, six-month retention) but the density feels slightly report-like. The original preference draft presents numbers cleanly but lacks comparative context — $19.40 and 81% are stated without baselines or trends.

criteria_analysis.name: Specificity Over Sentimentality
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is loaded with non-decorative specifics: Tuesday in February, forklift/OSHA 10/GED certifications, $22/hour, $400 stipend, regional distribution hub, health coverage starting day one — each detail reveals character or stakes. The generic draft uses template-style language ('simply needed the right door opened') and Maria's story is thin on particulars. The original preference draft has some good specifics ('logistics coordinator role,' 'saving for his first apartment') but 'mornings that feel different' leans sentimental, and the details are less dense and particular.

per_criterion.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels here — Marisol walked in, carried, researched, identified, decided; the organization appears only in supporting clauses ('what your investment made possible was the coordinator'). The generic draft opens with 'we placed 847 individuals,' centering the organization as hero and framing participants as placed objects. The original preference draft is strong — Marcus 'walked into,' 'was six months into' — but 'we placed 247 people' in the second paragraph centers the organization as actor.

The generic draft never addresses the donor at all. The generic draft has no call to action at all. The original preference draft includes a timeframe (December 31st) and ties back to Marcus, but uses 'a gift of any size' rather than a specific amount, and the impact number (300 placements) is aspirational rather than tied to a specific funding threshold.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft satisfies every criterion at or near the highest level — it nails agency in language, donor framing as strategic decision-maker with causal mechanisms, narrative-first structure with clean widening, a specific and story-tied call to action, contextual data integration, and concrete specificity throughout. The original preference draft is competent and shares many of the user's instincts but falls short on donor framing, data context, and call-to-action specificity. The generic draft fails most criteria — no donor address, organization-centered language, no CTA, and thin narrative detail.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both drafts communicate their messages with precision and transparency. Each presents data clearly and contextualizes it with a human story.

Draft A moves seamlessly from individual story to systemic impact to data to ask, with each paragraph building logically on the last. Draft B flows well but covers similar ground more compactly, slightly limiting the logical development between ideas.
Both drafts are mechanically clean with no grammatical errors or typos.

Draft A has a clear four-paragraph architecture — story, attribution of impact, broader data, call to action — that gives the reader a well-paced journey. Draft B, as a single paragraph, compresses everything together, which limits structural clarity.

Draft A's specificity (the folder, Tuesday in February, $400 stipend, forklift certification) creates a vivid, textured narrative that pulls the reader in. Draft B's story of Maria is effective but more generic and less immersive in its details.

Draft A strikes a notably effective balance — confident and respectful without being sentimental, treating the subject with agency rather than as a passive beneficiary. Draft B's tone is appropriate and professional but slightly more conventional in its fundraising voice.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger piece: it offers richer specificity, a more deliberate structure, a more distinctive voice, and a sharper call to action, all while treating its subject with notable agency and respect. Draft B is competent and effective but reads as a solid single paragraph rather than a fully developed, architecturally considered appeal.

suggestion_text:
- **Agency & Power in Language**: This criterion is working exactly as intended — the rubric-guided draft scored highest and the user explicitly named Marisol's subject-position as the reason she won. Kept unchanged.
- **Donor Addressed as Strategic Decision-Maker**: This criterion is working exactly as intended — the rubric-guided draft scored highest (5 vs. 3 vs. 1) and the user's comments directly validated the causal mechanism and decision framing as the reason the rubric-guided draft was the clear winner. Kept unchanged.
- **Specific, Story-Tied Call to Action**: This criterion is working exactly as intended — the rubric-guided draft scored highest (5 vs. 3 vs. 1) and the user called out the specific dollar amount, participant count, and story callback as decisive. Kept unchanged.
- **Specificity Over Sentimentality**: This criterion is working exactly as intended — the rubric-guided draft scored highest (5 vs. 3 vs. 2) and the user explicitly called out deficit framing ('hadn't held steady work'), sentimental lines ('mornings that feel different'), and template language ('the right door opened') as reasons the other drafts ranked lower. Added a new dimension flagging deficit-framing as a specific failure mode, since the user's comments identified it as a distinct problem the existing dimensions don't explicitly name.

suggested_rubric.name: Agency & Power in Language
suggested_rubric.description: Community members, local partners, and program participants are the grammatical subjects of sentences, doing active verbs — they enrolled, chose, built, pursued. The organization appears in supporting clauses, not as the hero. The subject of the sentence encodes who holds power, and this user treats it as non-negotiable.

dimensions.label: Community members and local partners are the grammatical subjects of key sentences, not the organization
Participants are described with active verbs (enrolled, chose, walked, built, decided) rather than passive constructions or verbs of reception
The organization is positioned as enabler or supporter of community-driven action, not the primary actor
No sentences frame participants as things that programs happened to — literacy is something they pursued, not something delivered to them

suggested_rubric.name: Donor Addressed as Strategic Decision-Maker
suggested_rubric.description: The donor is spoken to directly throughout the piece — not just at the opening or closing. Their role is framed around their decision to invest, not their generosity. They should feel like a strategic partner in what happened: someone whose specific choice enabled community capacity through a tangible operational mechanism, not someone who wrote a check.

dimensions.label: The donor is addressed in second person ('you') at multiple points in the body of the piece, not only at the beginning or end
Language frames the donor's contribution around their decision or investment, not their generosity or kindness
The donor is positioned as enabling what community members already knew how to do — never as rescuing, saving, or giving to passive recipients
The connection between the donor's gift and a specific outcome is explained through a tangible operational detail — how the funding created the conditions, not just that it did
The donor's previous investment is connected to a specific, named current result — not just thanked in general terms

suggested_rubric.name: Narrative-First Structure
suggested_rubric.description: The piece opens with one specific person's story — fully, without any statistics — before the lens widens to show scale. The reader meets a real person and sits with their choices before encountering any numbers. Numbers arrive afterward as evidence that this is not just one story. This sequencing holds regardless of format — sectioned newsletters, continuous prose, or letters.

dimensions.label: The piece opens with a specific individual — named, situated, making a choice — before any programmatic framing
No enrollment figures, program statistics, or scale numbers appear until after the opening narrative is complete
The individual story and the scale numbers are sequentially separated — the reader finishes one person's moment before the camera pulls back, whether through section breaks or paragraph transitions
The transition from individual story to aggregate numbers creates a widening effect ('she's not alone') rather than a topic shift into reporting mode

suggested_rubric.name: Specific, Story-Tied Call to Action
suggested_rubric.description: The ask connects directly to the narrative — a specific funding amount, a specific number of people, a specific timeframe — not a generic appeal. The reader should be able to draw a straight line from the opening story to what their next gift would do. The ask feels earned by the narrative, not appended to it, and frames continued giving as a natural extension of an existing partnership.

dimensions.label: The ask includes a specific dollar amount or funding threshold
The ask names a specific number of people or families it would reach
The ask includes an honest, concrete timeframe for when the impact would be felt
The ask explicitly connects back to the person or moment from the opening narrative — through direct reference, structural echo, or closing callback — so the reader sees the thread
The ask frames the next gift as a natural next step in an ongoing partnership, not as a new or separate transaction

suggested_rubric.name: Data Integration Without Losing Voice
suggested_rubric.description: When program outcomes or harder statistics are included, they are presented in plain, direct sentences that maintain the narrative voice. Each data point does specific work — revealing trajectory, stakes, or scope — rather than being listed for comprehensiveness. The writing does not shift into report or institutional register when numbers appear.

dimensions.label: Statistics are presented in narrative sentences that maintain the piece's voice, not in tables, charts, or bullet-point report format
Key metrics include context — comparisons, baselines, or trends — so the reader understands significance, not just magnitude
Each data point serves a specific narrative function (establishing scale, showing trajectory, revealing stakes) rather than being included for comprehensiveness
The transition into and out of data-heavy passages does not shift the writing into institutional, jargon-heavy, or report-style register

suggested_rubric.name: Specificity Over Sentimentality
suggested_rubric.description: Impact is conveyed through concrete, real details — names, moments, choices, places, times, logistical particulars — rather than through adjectives, emotional language, or template-style warmth. Every detail should pull narrative weight; specificity serves both emotional resonance and credibility simultaneously. The piece should feel like it could only have been written about these people.

dimensions.label: Key claims are grounded in specific, verifiable details (names, times, places, logistical particulars) rather than vague or general language
The piece could not describe a different program without substantial rewriting — it is particular to its subject
Emotional moments arise from specific facts or narrative details, not from adjectives or declarative emotional statements
Each specific detail does narrative work — revealing character, stakes, or choices — rather than being decorative
Realities, limitations, and program status are framed accurately rather than glossed over or inflated
Participants are not introduced through what they lacked or had lost — the opening situates them by their choices, resources, or goals, not their hardship or absence
